summary: French Rococo painter who pioneered the fete galante genre. Known for The Embarkation for Cythera, Gersaint's Shop Sign, and Gilles (Pierrot). Depicted commedia dell'arte characters and scenes of aristocrats in outdoor settings.

works.name: The Embarkation for Cythera (1717)
works.description: Fete galante painting showing couples preparing to depart the island sacred to Venus/Cupid, surrounded by swarms of putti/cherubs near a golden gondola. Two versions exist: one in the Louvre, one in Berlin's Charlottenburg Palace.

clues.clue: couples preparing to depart/arrive at the island where Venus was born, surrounded by putti/cherubs near a golden gondola
examples: painted several cupids flying in the air as a procession of couples approaches a golden gondola
joyful putti flying around a convoy of lovers who are about to board a boat which will take them to the island where Venus was born

clues.clue: two versions: Louvre original and Charlottenburg Palace version (Berlin) with a mast in background
examples: Berlin's Charlottenburg Palace contains the second version of one of this artist's paintings which, unlike the first hanging in the Louvre, contains a mast in the background

clues.clue: typical example of fete galante genre/theme

clues.clue: man in red with a staff on a hill (in both versions)

clues.clue: bust of Venus decorated with a garland of roses
examples: showing a bust of Venus decorated with a garland of roses

clues.clue: debate over whether figures are leaving or arriving at the island
examples: There is some debate over whether the figures in the painting are leaving or entering this place

works.name: Gersaint's Shop Sign (L'Enseigne de Gersaint)
works.description: Watteau's last major painting before his death from tuberculosis. Depicts an art dealer's shop where workers pack away a portrait of Louis XIV.

works.name: Mezzetin

works.description: French Rococo artist. Pioneered the fete galante genre. Taught by Claude Gillot. Student Jean-Baptiste Pater. Used trois-crayons technique. Died of tuberculosis.

comprehensive_summary:
Antoine Watteau was a French Rococo painter who pioneered the fete galante genre, a term created by the French Academy in 1717 specifically to categorize his paintings of fancily dressed aristocrats in imaginary outdoor settings. His most famous painting, The Embarkation for Cythera, shows couples preparing to depart the island sacred to Venus, surrounded by swarms of putti near a golden gondola. Two versions exist: the original in the Louvre and a second in Berlin's Charlottenburg Palace, which includes a mast in the background. Both versions show a man in red with a staff on a hill. There is scholarly debate over whether the figures are leaving or arriving at the island.

Gersaint's Shop Sign was Watteau's last major painting before his death from tuberculosis. It depicts an art dealer's shop where workers pack a portrait of Louis XIV into a wooden box while customers admire artworks. It was intended as a trade sign for the art dealer Edme Gersaint. Gilles (or Pierrot) depicts a forlorn-looking man in a white commedia dell'arte costume with pink or red bowties on his shoes, with Pantalon riding a donkey visible in the background.

Watteau frequently painted commedia dell'arte characters, including Mezzetin, shown playing guitar with a backwards-facing female statue behind him. Several related paintings feature musical themes: The Scale of Love, The Love Lesson, and The Love Song all feature guitar players and sometimes include a bust (possibly of Pythagoras). He used the trois-crayons (three chalks) technique for drawings. He was taught by Claude Gillot and studied under Claude Audran, curator of the Luxembourg Palace, where he copied scenes from Rubens's Marie de' Medici cycle. His student Jean-Baptiste Pater also worked in the fete galante genre. Other notable works include Ceres (the only surviving painting from his four seasons series), The Pleasures of the Dance, and The Surprise. Jean-Baptiste Carpeaux later sculpted a monument to Watteau.
